name: Lyre of Orpheus
description: The Lyre of Orpheus was no ordinary musical instrument; it was a divine artifact capable of enchanting all living things. Crafted by either Apollo, the god of music and light, or Hermes, the messenger god and inventor, and given to Orpheus, the Thracian poet and musician, this lyre possessed the power to charm gods, men, beasts, and even inanimate objects. Its music could soothe the savage Cerberus, halt the flow of rivers, and coax trees to dance. Orpheus's skill with the lyre was legendary; his melodies could evoke profound emotions, inspire courage, and bring solace to the grieving. Most famously, he used the lyre's enchanting power to attempt to retrieve his beloved Eurydice from the Underworld, charming Hades and Persephone with his sorrowful songs. Although his attempt ultimately failed due to his impatience, the lyre remained a testament to the power of music and love. After Orpheus's tragic death, the lyre was said to have been placed among the stars by Zeus or Apollo, becoming the constellation Lyra, a celestial reminder of the poet's extraordinary gift and enduring legacy. The lyre embodies the power of art to transcend mortal limitations and touch the divine.
subtitle: The enchanting instrument of the poet
origin: Crafted by Apollo or Hermes and given to Orpheus.

keyMyths.title: Orpheus and Eurydice
keyMyths.description: Orpheus used his lyre to charm Hades and Persephone in an attempt to bring his deceased wife Eurydice back from the Underworld. He almost succeeded, but his lack of faith caused him to lose her forever.

keyMyths.title: Orpheus's Death
keyMyths.description: After failing to retrieve Eurydice, Orpheus was torn apart by Maenads, followers of Dionysus, because he refused to worship any god but Apollo. His lyre was then placed among the stars.

sources.author: Ovid
sources.description: A narrative poem recounting myths and legends.
sources.text: A narrative poem recounting myths and legends.
